It's a seven, seven years.
I'm not going to put the plus to age myself, but.
Amazing.
Yeah, that's, that's really great.
I was asking because we, I'm glad you called it an engagement manager.

It's okay.

So I've been a growthx for about seven months.
I lead customer operations, which means that I'm sort of organizing the delivery team that is responsible for all of the literal deliverables for our clients.
So contractual obligations, et cetera, et cetera.
I'm also the direct, so I'm like kind of coordinating the ops, but also the.
that's good.
So,
Engagement managers are the relationship owner.

That doesn't mean that they shouldn't have like an editorial background, a sense of what makes content good, and the ability to like talk to SEO strategy, like kind of product positioning, brand, things like that.
But their main focus is really to be the kind of point person, and core, like customer contact.
A little bit of project management, but like, really the trusted expert for the client, and then coordinating the work to kind of where we're going.
There's also a director layer on top of the engagement managers, not directly managing them, but our director model is pooled.
So directors are strategic experts.
They've spent time a lot of times like in house building big brands, things like that.

And so they'll be running like SEO audits, if we need to shift to content strategy, they're running like AI, sort of like thinking about AI visibility, and how we really structure and change the direction for clients when they're not seeing performance.
So those are kind of the three layers in the delivery org.
And an engagement manager will manage eight accounts today, but probably 12 by the end of the first quarter of 2026.
That makes sense.
So that's growing pretty quickly.
And yeah, it's just like you will be owning account health, essentially, and everything that means for a marketing agency.
Perfect.
So yeah, what you described, I realized there's a lot with the new org system, it's very aligned with the organizational structure of an agency.
It's very efficient, it flows really well.
Well, from all the roles that you described and how they operate off of each other.
And so I think going back to, I guess, my experience with it, it's very much in tune with everything you're expected for it.
For me, as far as accounts that I've managed, max I've managed is 12.
Midsize and prize accounts are primarily what I've been doing for the past five years.
So 12 accounts.
I have been that main point of contact that has managed the projects internally with the team to ensure we got our deliverables ready to go.
Before that, I set my time, my schedule project by, including QA, because nothing gets sent to the client without me taking a look at it.
So I do have that editorial background to know what is good, what the client is seeking.
And if I have to have that back and forth with my team member, I have no problem doing so as well to ensure that the deliverable is up to the client's standard.
As far as client relationships goes, as far as contractual renewals, that is...
I bread and butter.
I love contract renewal time.
I utilize my reporting as I use quantitative metrics to back my argument for everything.
Numbers speak to it all.
And so for me, I utilize the QBRs that I present to clients as kind of a reference point to do my quarterly check-ins with the health always.
So there is no surprise come end of year, whether it's them saying, oh, we don't want to continue.
And I can ask them as to why, because according to the QBRs, this stat on the 3rd has been stated for an express by you.
And so I just want to see where may have been a disconnect.
Or for me, if it's internal, as far as we haven't been hitting those marks as far as performance goes, there's no surprise for our team as well, far as whether it's the director or yourself, Andi.
And so I make sure there's a clear through line and communication externally and internally.
And I create a clear track list.
So for me, as far as visibility goes with client dynamics, setting up the weekly or by-weekly.
Weekly meetings is a necessity.
Maria, Maria mentioned that there are pods.
So I said I have weekly pod meetings with each of my clients.
So if you're in my pod for said clients, we're meeting every week, two days before the client meeting.
So it allows us to align on what's taking place.
And you have 24 hours to make any sort of updates, because 24 hours prior to the client meeting, I will be sending them the agenda so they have time to at least throw in anything as well.

So there may be a wave of information that's coming in 24 hours prior to the meeting, but at least we are prepared for it or somewhat prepared for it to the best of our ability before meeting with the client, which helps create that frictionless experience, as frictionless as possible.
I can't skip that final word and finish frictionless.
And so through that, I measure client health through there.
I meet with my leaders once a week as well, whether it's my director or yourself.
Andi, I meet once a week just to give full downloads on what's going on in all my accounts.
So there's full visibility through and through.
If there is an escalation, you will be made aware of it immediately.
As far as speaking to SEO, I am very involved in it.

And because we're using Looker dashboards today.

Okay.

I'm used

With local SEO, I wanted to say I'm familiar with it because I've had clients who have multiple locations.
So I was curious, like, how that works with your clients as well, but I'll leave that for the questionnaire, like, for my questions afterwards, because I feel like that's minutiae details.

If I go on, like, SEMrush or Screaming Frog, I will, because I know how to utilize said platforms and, like, the usage of them.
But I can definitely speak to it on a client front where I make sure it's very elementary and digestible and easy to understand.
Cool.
Can you talk to me about an example of an account that you've managed that required you to coordinate with multiple players on your team?

So I can start with, no, I'll do Watts Water.

And I get it.

So I said, you know what?
I hear you.
Please, if you'd allow me, let me look at your Power BI.

And so he
He notified leadership on his end about this document.
So we knew this was our source of truth at this point with analyzing revenue based off of the channels that we're putting money into, right?
Or even organically.
And so through there, I saw some of the gaps where, you know, SEO kind of helps with paid media.

was three months.
